etymology.description: semantic 金(金) (jīn, "metal") + phonetic 旁(旁) (páng) -> metal currency -> pound
etymology.interpretation: 鎊(bàng, "pound") combines 金(金) (jīn, "metal"), indicating a metallic object or currency, with 旁(旁) (páng) as the phonetic component to provide the pronunciation. The meaning of 'pound' arises from the context of a unit of currency, traditionally minted in metal.
etymology.simplification: 鎊(bàng, "pound") was simplified to 镑(bàng) by replacing the 金(金) "metal" radical with 钅(钅) (jīn), the simplified form of the metal radical, to reduce strokes while retaining the phonetic 旁(旁) (páng).

examples.chinese: 我给了他一百英镑(我給了他一百英鎊)。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ gěi le tā yī bǎi yīngbàng.
examples.english: I gave him one hundred pounds.

examples.chinese: 这把古董剑重十磅(這把古董劍重十鎊)。
examples.pinyin: Zhè bǎ gǔdǒng jiàn zhòng shí bàng.
examples.english: This antique sword weighs ten pounds.

examples.chinese: 他们刚买了一台重达两百磅的钢琴(他們剛買了一台重達兩百鎊的鋼琴)。
examples.pinyin: Tāmen gāng mǎi le yī tái zhòng dá liǎng bǎi bàng de gāngqín.
examples.english: They just bought a piano that weighs two hundred pounds.
